preamble: The shift from a lateral consonant [l] (or lateral fricative [ɬ]) to a sibilant [s] is best documented in the Semitic language family, where Proto-Semitic *ś, reconstructed as a voiceless lateral fricative [ɬ], merged with the sibilant [s] in Hebrew and with [ʃ] in Arabic. This change reflects the instability of lateral fricatives cross-linguistically: the lateral airflow is acoustically similar to sibilant turbulence, making lateral-to-sibilant shifts a natural lenition pathway when the lateral distinction is lost.

examples.note: Proto-Semitic *ś was a voiceless alveolar lateral fricative [ɬ], preserved in Modern South Arabian languages such as Mehri. In Hebrew, this sound merged with /s/, written as שׂ (sin, with a left dot) to distinguish it from שׁ (shin [ʃ]). The Greek loanword balsamon (from Hebrew בָּשָׂם bāśām 'balsam') preserves the lateral quality of the Hebrew word via its /l/ in the Greek form, providing direct evidence for the earlier [ɬ] pronunciation.

The emphatic counterpart *ṣ́ [ɬʼ] merged with the emphatic sibilant [tsʼ]/[sʼ] in Hebrew (tsade). Arabic generalized the same sound as ض /dˤ/, the so-called 'dad', famously called 'the most difficult consonant' by Arab grammarians. The 8th-century grammarian Sibawayh's description of Arabic ض as a lateral-contact sound provides independent evidence for the lateral origin.

While Hebrew merged *ś [ɬ] with [s], Arabic merged it with [ʃ]. This parallel but distinct outcome from the same Proto-Semitic source illustrates the sibilant-lateral interface: both [s] and [ʃ] are possible targets for a defricativizing lateral. E.g., Proto-Semitic *ś-m-s (sun) > Hebrew שֶׁמֶשׁ šemeš vs. Arabic شَمْس šams — both from the same lateral fricative root.

examples.to: Ugaritic ṯ [θ] or š [ʃ]; Phoenician merged into [ʃ]
examples.note: In Ugaritic, the reflex of *ś was sometimes written as ṯ (a separate letter), suggesting a dental/lateral quality distinct from plain [s]. In Phoenician and derived alphabets, *ś merged with [ʃ], and this merged letter ש was borrowed into Greek as sigma Σ. The diversification of reflexes (s, ʃ, θ) across Semitic languages underscores the instability of the original lateral fricative.

In Akkadian (the oldest attested Semitic language, written in cuneiform from c. 2600 BCE), Proto-Semitic *ś merged with š [ʃ], parallel to Arabic. E.g., PS *śamš (sun) > Akkadian šamšu. Akkadian lost the lateral fricative very early, merging it entirely into the sibilant series.

sources: Steiner, R.C. (1977). The Case for Fricative-Laterals in Proto-Semitic. American Oriental Society.
Huehnergard, J. (2004). Proto-Semitic Language and Culture. In The American Heritage Dictionary of the English Language, Appendix II.
Blau, J. (2010). Phonology and Morphology of Biblical Hebrew. Eisenbrauns.
